string.text:
The Stielhandgranate Model 1924, or M24, is a hand grenade manufactured and used by Nazi Germany from 1924 up until the end of the second world war. The grenade is activated by a string that the operator would pull downwards, which in return drags a steel rod through the igniter within the fuse.
 Many of these grenades found their way into the zone from massive WW2 stockpiles. They are favored by the lesser equipped factions due to their ease of throwing compared to conventional grenades, however some complain about the grenades length and the amount of time it takes to explode.
 %c[ui_gray_3]CHARACTERISTICS:
 %c[d_purple] • %c[ui_gray_2] Blast power: %c[ui_gray_1]medium
 %c[d_purple] • %c[ui_gray_2] Blast radius: %c[ui_gray_1]2-8 m
 %c[d_purple] • %c[ui_gray_2] Avg. number of fragments: %c[ui_gray_1]200 (small shrapnels)
 %c[d_purple] • %c[ui_gray_2] Explosion delay: %c[ui_gray_1]5.5 seconds

The Stielhandgranate Model 1924, or M24, is a hand grenade manufactured and used by Nazi Germany from 1924 up until the end of the second world war. The grenade is activated by a string that the operator would pull downwards, which in return drags a steel rod through the igniter within the fuse.
 The Splitterring was a fragmentation sleeve for the M24 and M43 stick grenades, developed by the Heer in 1942. German stick grenades had only a thin steel casing surrounding the explosive charge, which relied principally on blast for effect; the addition of a Splitterring gave it greatly increased anti-personnel fragmentation ability.
 %c[ui_gray_3]CHARACTERISTICS:
 %c[d_purple] • %c[ui_gray_2] Blast power: %c[ui_gray_1]medium
 %c[d_purple] • %c[ui_gray_2] Blast radius: %c[ui_gray_1]6-12 m
 %c[d_purple] • %c[ui_gray_2] Avg. number of fragments: %c[ui_gray_1]500 (small shrapnels)
 %c[d_purple] • %c[ui_gray_2] Explosion delay: %c[ui_gray_1]4.0 seconds

The Stielhandgranate Model 1924, or M24, is a hand grenade manufactured and used by Nazi Germany from 1924 up until the end of the second world war. The grenade is activated by a string that the operator would pull downwards, which in return drags a steel rod through the igniter within the fuse.
 The M24 lacked effectiveness against hard targets such as tanks and buildings. A common solution was an improvised Geballte Ladung or "bundle charge". Up to six heads of M24 grenades – handles and fuses removed – would be strapped around a complete grenade, quickly improvising a grenade with up to seven times the explosive power. However, the added weight made it more difficult to throw, and the increased size meant that it was not practical to carry with one hand and that far fewer could be carried.
 %c[ui_gray_3]CHARACTERISTICS:
 %c[d_purple] • %c[ui_gray_2] Blast power: %c[ui_gray_1]large
 %c[d_purple] • %c[ui_gray_2] Blast radius: %c[ui_gray_1]10-16 m
 %c[d_purple] • %c[ui_gray_2] Avg. number of fragments: %c[ui_gray_1]650 (small shrapnels)
 %c[d_purple] • %c[ui_gray_2] Explosion delay: %c[ui_gray_1]4.5 seconds
